metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the test file from `tests/test_plan_review_triage.py` to `tests/unit/test_plan_review_triage.py` (matching the project convention) and update all four AC pytest commands accordingly.
evidence: Plan scope: `tests/test_plan_review_triage.py — unit tests (see ACs)`

Project convention confirmed by grep: tests/unit/test_plan_review.py, tests/unit/test_plan_review_novelty.py, tests/unit/test_plan_review_convergence.py, tests/unit/test_plan_review_completion.py, tests/unit/test_plan_review_rising_floor.py, tests/unit/test_plan_review_overlap.py — all under tests/unit/
AC proving commands all reference `tests/test_plan_review_triage.py` — will fail if placed at root tests/ level
findings.finding: The test file is placed at `tests/test_plan_review_triage.py` but every existing plan-review unit test lives under `tests/unit/` (e.g., `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py`, `tests/unit/test_plan_review_novelty.py`, `tests/unit/test_plan_review_convergence.py`). The plan's path drops the `unit/` subdirectory, so the file will be created in the wrong location and all four AC proving commands (`python -m pytest tests/test_plan_review_triage.py -k <marker> -q`) will fail with a file-not-found / collection error.
findings.location: Scope section: `tests/test_plan_review_triage.py` and AC lines 1–4 (all four pytest proving commands)
scenarios: `make test` / `pytest` discovery passes over `tests/unit/` but not over the wrong location, so the new tests are silently skipped
All four AC proving commands fail with a 'no such file or directory' collection error, making the ACs unverifiable
findings.suggested_fix: Change `tests/test_plan_review_triage.py` → `tests/unit/test_plan_review_triage.py` in the Scope section and update every AC proving command from `python -m pytest tests/test_plan_review_triage.py` to `python -m pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review_triage.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that exercises the `workflow_ops.py` integration: run a minimal Pass-4 coach step with a fixed set of advisory and blocking findings and assert that the returned verdict's coaching section contains a `triage` array with the correct bucket assignments (e.g. `assert result['coaching']['triage'][0]['bucket'] in ('apply-now', 'defer')`).
evidence: Scope: 'invoke `triage_advisories` on the surviving advisory findings in the Pass-4 coach step and attach the resulting `triage` array to the step output'
ACs 1–4 proving commands all target `test_plan_review_triage.py` (the pure-function unit tests)
AC6: `make lint && make typecheck && make test` — no assertion that the coaching section of the returned verdict contains the `triage` key
ABSENCE: no AC says 'when the Pass-4 coach step runs with advisory findings, the returned verdict's coaching section contains a `triage` array with the correct entries'
findings.finding: The scope explicitly modifies `workflow_ops.py` to invoke `triage_advisories` and attach the resulting `triage` array to the Pass-4 step output, but no AC exercises this integration path. All four unit-test ACs (AC1–AC4) test `triage_advisories` as a pure function in isolation; AC6's `make test` green-pass may not cover the `workflow_ops.py` integration if existing tests don't exercise the Pass-4 coach step with advisory findings.
findings.location: ## Scope — `workflow_ops.py` bullet; ## Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
scenarios: An implementor writes `triage_advisories` correctly but wires it to the wrong output key in `workflow_ops.py`, or forgets to call it at all — all unit tests pass, `make test` is green, and the integration gap ships undetected.
The `triage` array is attached to a nested dict that the caller never reads — no AC would catch this.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'When `run_pass4_coach` (or equivalent `workflow_ops.py` entry point) is called with a mix of blocking and advisory decided findings, the returned result dict contains `result["coaching"]["triage"]` as a non-empty list with every entry having keys `id`, `criteria`, `priority`, `block_threshold`, `bucket`, `reason`; blocking findings are absent. Proving command: `python -m pytest tests/test_plan_review_triage.py -k integration -q`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that verifies the end-to-end integration: given a mock plan-review Pass-4 run with at least one advisory finding, the returned verdict's coaching section contains a `triage` list with one entry per advisory finding.
evidence: Scope: 'workflow_ops.py — invoke `triage_advisories` on the surviving advisory findings in the Pass-4 coach step and attach the resulting `triage` array to the step output that assembles the returned verdict's coaching section'
What: 'This array is attached to the plan-review RESULT returned to the caller (under the coaching section)'
ABSENCE: No AC says 'given a full plan-review run, the returned result contains a `triage` key under the coaching section with one entry per advisory finding.'
findings.finding: The plan describes a workflow integration deliverable — invoking `triage_advisories` in workflow_ops.py's Pass-4 coach step and attaching the resulting `triage` array to the returned verdict's coaching section — but no AC directly verifies that this integration wire-up is present and correct in the returned result. ACs 1–4 test the pure function in isolation; AC6 checks lint/typecheck/test green, but none asserts that the workflow actually returns a `triage` key in its coaching output.
findings.location: Scope (workflow_ops.py bullet) and What section (triage attached to plan-review RESULT) vs ACs 1–6
scenarios: An implementer could add `triage_advisories` as a standalone function passing all unit tests while forgetting to wire it into workflow_ops.py; AC6's `make test` would still pass if only the unit tests exist.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or augment AC5's proving command with separate grep anchors for each required documentation element, e.g. `grep -q "APPLY_NOW_MARGIN" docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -q "apply-now" docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -q "R7" docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -q "defer" docs/plan-review-gate.md`, or use a single structured section heading check plus content assertions.
evidence: AC5 text: 'documents the triage stage, its ranking rule (the `APPLY_NOW_MARGIN` default, the ordering, the defer-reason format), and the R7 dogfood expectation'
Proving command: `grep -q "APPLY_NOW_MARGIN" docs/plan-review-gate.md`
Defect taxonomy #1: substring false-positive — the grep anchors on one token out of four required documentation elements
findings.finding: AC5's proving command (`grep -q "APPLY_NOW_MARGIN" docs/plan-review-gate.md`) proves only that the string 'APPLY_NOW_MARGIN' appears somewhere in the file, but the AC requires documentation of four distinct elements: (1) the triage stage, (2) the ranking rule including the APPLY_NOW_MARGIN default, (3) the ordering and defer-reason format, and (4) the R7 dogfood expectation. A single grep hit satisfies the command but leaves elements 1, 3, and 4 entirely unverified.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC5
scenarios: An implementor adds a single sentence mentioning APPLY_NOW_MARGIN but omits the ordering rule, defer-reason format, and R7 expectation — AC5's proving command passes, but three of the four documentation requirements are unmet.
A reviewer signs off based on the grep passing, missing that the dogfood rationale tying R6 to R7 is absent from the docs.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single grep with: `grep -qE 'APPLY_NOW_MARGIN.*0\.10' docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -q 'apply-now' docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -q 'R7' docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -qE 'deferred:.*block' docs/plan-review-gate.md` — each grep anchors one of the four stated documentation requirements.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name and confirm the additive-safety of the returned verdict's coaching section for all callers (orchestrator, agent consumer, any schema layer) — or add `workflow_ops.py` caller survey to Scope and an AC proving the agent actually receives the `triage` field end-to-end.
evidence: Plan states sidecar.build_payload is unchanged and enumerates fields explicitly — persistence path is safe.
Plan states triage is returned to the caller under the coaching section — but no caller survey is provided.
Absence: no statement that the coaching-section schema is open/additive or that the orchestrator/agent ignores unknown keys.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `triage` field to the returned plan-review verdict's coaching section but does not acknowledge downstream callers of that returned result (beyond sidecar.build_payload, which is explicitly unchanged) — specifically whether the orchestrator, agent consumer, or any schema-validation layer expects a fixed set of keys in the coaching section and would break or silently mishandle the new field.
findings.location: ## What — 'This array is attached to the plan-review RESULT returned to the caller (under the coaching section)' and 'NOT added to the persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecar'
scenarios: If workflow_ops.py assembles the coaching section into a typed dataclass or TypedDict with explicit fields, adding `triage` requires a schema update not listed in Scope.
If the agent or orchestrator iterates the coaching section's keys and dispatches on them, an unexpected `triage` key could cause a KeyError or be silently dropped, undermining the feature.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the What section naming the coaching-section type (e.g. 'the coaching section is a plain dict; the agent consumes it by key lookup, so adding `triage` is additive-safe') and add an AC or integration test asserting the returned verdict contains `triage` with the expected structure when advisories are present.

findings.finding: The plan introduces a triage list for advisory findings that received NO coach move ('advisories that mapped to no coach move still appear in the triage list keyed by finding id'), but never specifies the verdict dict key where this list is placed, nor the object shape of entries in it. The existing verdict shape (finalize_verdict in orchestrator.py) has no `triage` key, and the plan names no new top-level key to add.
findings.location: What section: 'advisories that mapped to no coach move still appear in the triage list keyed by finding id'; Scope: passes.py / workflow_ops.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the leaf's `file_impact` metadata (which records `orchestrator.py` as "MOVE_REGISTRY addition" and `review_kernel/coach.py` as "triage move seam") with the leaf's written Scope (which excludes both files), and update the parent epic's description or add an explicit note acknowledging that R6's mechanism was changed from the SOTA-specified MOVE_REGISTRY entry to an out-of-registry pure function — so the parent's plan and the leaf's stored metadata agree with the leaf's actual implementation scope.
evidence: Leaf file_impact: {"path": "src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py", "reason": "MOVE_REGISTRY addition"} and {"path": "src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py", "reason": "triage move seam"}
Leaf Scope: 'NOT touched: sidecar.py / the plan_review_result_v2 schema (triage is returned, not persisted) and review_kernel/coach.py (kernel mechanism unchanged)'
Leaf What: 'It is NOT an entry in the Pass-4 MOVE_REGISTRY (in passes.py, re-exported by orchestrator.py)'
SOTA §R6: 'Add a move to the registry (rendered deterministically, like all moves): Triage each surviving advisory into the plan before claiming'
Parent epic Context: 'R6=5dac (advisory-triage move, ship-first)' — sources R6's definition from the SOTA document
findings.finding: The leaf's ticket metadata (`file_impact`) records `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` (reason: "MOVE_REGISTRY addition") and `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py` (reason: "triage move seam") as impacted files — but the leaf's own Scope section explicitly declares these files NOT touched, and the What section states the triage is "NOT an entry in the Pass-4 `MOVE_REGISTRY`." The parent epic sources R6's definition from the SOTA document (§R6), which specifies the mechanism as "Add a move to the registry (rendered deterministically, like all moves)" — i.e., a MOVE_REGISTRY entry touching exactly those two files. The leaf has redefined the deliverable (from a registry-entry to an out-of-registry pure function) relative to the parent's sourcing document without the parent's own plan being updated to reflect that change.
findings.location: Leaf Scope section ('NOT touched … review_kernel/coach.py') vs. leaf file_impact metadata ('MOVE_REGISTRY addition', 'triage move seam') vs. parent epic Context ('R6 … deterministic pass-4 coaching move') and SOTA §R6 ('Add a move to the registry')
scenarios: A reviewer or downstream story author reads the parent epic's SOTA reference and expects a MOVE_REGISTRY entry; the leaf delivers an out-of-registry pure function with a different integration point and trigger semantics — this can cause confusion about which files actually change and whether the registry's per-finding template contract was intentionally bypassed.

The leaf's stale file_impact metadata causes the commit-ticket gate and any file-impact-based conflict detection to expect changes to orchestrator.py and coach.py that will never arrive, potentially flagging a false conflict with R7 or other stories that touch those files.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the leaf's `file_impact` to remove the stale `orchestrator.py` and `coach.py` entries (replacing them with the actual files: `passes.py`, `workflow_ops.py`, `tests/test_plan_review_triage.py`, `docs/plan-review-gate.md`). Add a sentence to the leaf's Why/Mechanism section explicitly noting the departure from the SOTA's MOVE_REGISTRY prescription and why the pure-function approach is preferred — this surfaces the deliberate reuse-vs-isolation tradeoff rather than leaving it as an implicit contradiction. Optionally, add a note in the parent epic's integration comment that R6's mechanism was revised from registry-entry to pure function.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact attachment point for the `triage` array in the returned verdict (e.g., a new top-level key `triage` on the dict returned by `workflow_ops.plan_review_coach`), and state explicitly whether `plan_review_verdict`'s schema is extended to include it or whether `triage` is intentionally outside the validated envelope (e.g., added after `validate_structured` or returned as a separate key on the step output alongside the verdict).
evidence: Plan: 'This array is attached to the plan-review RESULT returned to the caller (under the coaching section)'
Plan: 'sidecar.build_payload enumerates coaching-note fields explicitly … persistence is unnecessary and build_payload stays unchanged'
workflow_ops.py line 540: `return _findings.validate_structured(verdict, _OUTPUT_SCHEMA)` — verdict is validated against `plan_review_verdict` schema before return
orchestrator.py finalize_verdict (lines 531–543): `coaching` key is a list of rendered coach notes, not a dict with a `triage` sub-key
Absence: no plan statement that `plan_review_verdict` schema is amended to include a `triage` field
findings.finding: The plan attaches the `triage` array to the verdict 'under the coaching section' but `plan_review_coach` returns a dict validated against the `plan_review_verdict` output schema via `_findings.validate_structured(verdict, _OUTPUT_SCHEMA)`. The plan explicitly says the schema is NOT changed ('sidecar.build_payload stays unchanged; NOT added to the persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecar'), yet also says the array IS returned to the caller at review time. The actual attachment point — whether `triage` goes into the top-level verdict dict, inside the `coaching` list, inside `coverage`, or somewhere else — is never named, and the plan never explains how a `triage` field passes through `validate_structured` without a schema amendment.
findings.location: What § / 'Output shape (structured, returned — not persisted)' paragraph; workflow_ops.py `plan_review_coach` step (lines 483–540)
scenarios: Implementer attaches `triage` at the top-level verdict dict; `validate_structured` rejects or strips it as an unknown field, so the caller never sees the triage array.
Implementer attaches `triage` inside the `coaching` list entries; schema for coaching notes (sidecar.py lines 503–515) has no `triage` field, so the attachment point is still undefined.
Implementer skips `validate_structured` validation; the triage array leaks into the sidecar on next call to `sidecar.build_payload`, violating the plan's 'not persisted' invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Output shape section: 'The `triage` array is added as a top-level key `"triage"` on the dict returned by `plan_review_coach` AFTER `validate_structured` is called, so the schema-validated verdict is byte-identical to today and `triage` is accessible to the calling agent as `step.coach.triage`.' Or, if a schema extension is preferred, name it explicitly and add a corresponding AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the '0.60 under plan-review's routing' parenthetical to clarify that `block_threshold` is per-criterion (ranging from 0.60 to 0.95 across plan-review criteria) and that `triage_advisories` must read the per-finding `block_threshold` field — not assume a fixed 0.60 constant.
evidence: Plan text: 'block_threshold (the criterion blocking waterline; always set on the decided finding — decide.py, 0.60 under plan-review's routing, DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD=0.95 otherwise)'

criteria_routing.json shows block_threshold values: 0.60 (F1, E2, G5, COH, G6), 0.70 (T4, T1, T8, G1G2), 0.75 (E4), 0.95 (E1, E3, E5, E6, A1, G3, G4, G7, ISF, T2, T3, T5a-e, T6, T7, T9-T14, hedge, removal-rationale, and more)
decide.py line 342: block_threshold is always echoed from the per-call parameter, not a fixed 0.60 constant
findings.finding: The plan parenthetically asserts '0.60 under plan-review's routing' as the block_threshold value, but criteria_routing.json shows at least eight distinct block_threshold values used in plan-review (0.60 for F1/E2/G5/COH/G6, 0.70 for T4/T1/G1G2/T8, 0.75 for E4, 0.95 for E1/E3/E5/E6/A1/G3/G4/G7/ISF/T2/T3 and many others). An implementor reading the parenthetical as 'plan-review always uses 0.60' could (a) hardcode 0.60 instead of reading the per-finding block_threshold field, (b) produce wrong defer-reason strings (e.g. 'its 0.60 block line' for an E4 finding whose actual threshold is 0.75), or (c) produce wrong bucket assignments for the large majority of criteria whose threshold is 0.95.
findings.location: What / The ranking rule — '`block_threshold` (the criterion blocking waterline; always set on the decided finding — `decide.py`, 0.60 under plan-review's routing, `DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD=0.95` otherwise)'
scenarios: Implementor hardcodes 0.60 in the defer-reason string instead of reading the finding's block_threshold field — produces 'its 0.60 block line' for an E4 finding (threshold 0.75) or a T8 finding (threshold 0.70).

Implementor uses a constant 0.60 in the bucket condition instead of the per-finding field — misclassifies ~80% of advisory findings (those from criteria with 0.95 thresholds) as apply-now when they are defer.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '0.60 under plan-review's routing' with something like 'per-criterion, ranging 0.60–0.95 in plan-review's routing; always read from the finding's own `block_threshold` field'. Update the defer-reason format example to use a placeholder (e.g. `deferred: priority {priority} is {gap:.2f} below its {block_threshold} block line`) that makes clear it uses the per-finding value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `triage_advisories` handles a surviving advisory finding that has no `block_threshold` field (DET advisories are minted without it in `partition_findings`); either define a fallback value (e.g. `DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD`) or document that DET advisories are pre-classified as `defer` by convention, and add a test case for this path.
evidence: Plan states: 'block_threshold (the criterion blocking waterline; always set on the decided finding — decide.py, 0.60 under plan-review's routing, DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD=0.95 otherwise)'

orchestrator.py lines ~412-424: DET advisory findings are minted directly as {**f, 'id': ..., 'decision': 'advisory', 'severity': 'minor', 'priority': 0.4, 'validity': 1.0, 'impact': 0.4, 'tier': 'DET'} — no block_threshold key is stamped
decide.py pass3_decide: block_threshold is stamped ONLY inside this function; DET findings skip it entirely

If block_threshold is absent, triage_advisories bucketing rule `priority >= block_threshold - APPLY_NOW_MARGIN` will either raise KeyError or evaluate against 0/None, misclassifying every DET advisory as apply-now
findings.finding: DET advisory findings bypass `pass3_decide` and are minted without a `block_threshold` field, yet `triage_advisories` is specified to use `block_threshold` as the bucketing denominator — the plan's guarantee that `block_threshold` is 'always set on the decided finding' does not hold for this class of findings.
findings.location: What → Mechanism choice / Bucketing rule; also Scope → passes.py
scenarios: A round that surfaces one or more DET advisory findings (e.g. a ticket missing a progress note, which det_floor marks advisory) — these reach `surviving` without block_threshold, causing triage_advisories to behave incorrectly.
A mixed round with both LLM and DET advisories where the DET advisory has lower actual importance but is bucketed apply-now due to a missing threshold.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Bucketing rule: 'A finding without a `block_threshold` field (e.g. a DET-tier advisory) uses `DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD` (0.95) as its threshold, making it defer unless its priority also exceeds 0.85.' This is consistent with the fail-safe direction (DET advisories have a fixed priority of 0.4 and would always defer under 0.95, which matches their minor-severity nature).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either verify that R7's design (as committed or planned) supports deriving per-finding triage-bucket application from post-claim outcomes joined by `criteria`, or add a brief note acknowledging that R7's measurement granularity is TBD and the non-persistence decision should be revisited if R7 cannot make that join.
evidence: Plan: 'R7's dogfood metrics derive advisory application from post-claim outcomes joined to findings by criteria, not from a stored triage stamp — so persistence is unnecessary'

SotA R7 (docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md §R7): 'on every material post-claim description edit, link the edit to the ticket's latest REVIEW_RESULT and append a gate-eval record classifying it MISSED / CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED / UNKNOWABLE' — no mention of a criteria-join or triage-bucket differentiation
R7 is unshipped; its internal join design is not committed anywhere in the codebase
findings.finding: The plan's post-ship validation story depends on 'R7's advisory-application-latency metric' to validate the feature, but R7 is a separate, unshipped recommendation whose design is not yet committed. The plan asserts R7 'derive[s] advisory application from post-claim outcomes joined to findings by criteria, not from a stored triage stamp' — but R7's SotA description (§R7) does not specify a `criteria`-join design; it describes a coarser CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED classification. This is an unverified capability assertion about an as-yet-unbuilt downstream system.
findings.location: What section — 'It is NOT added to the persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecar'
scenarios: R7 is built without a criteria-join (as its own SotA spec implies — ticket-level CAUGHT-BUT-IGNORED classification, not finding-level), making it unable to distinguish triage bucket (apply-now vs defer) from advisory application. The plan's justification for not persisting triage then rests on a capability R7 doesn't have, and the feature cannot be dogfooded as promised.

findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py` is an unenumerated consumer: its `build_payload()` explicitly enumerates coaching-note fields (move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs, coaching, decision) with individual `.get()` calls, so a new `triage`/`triage_reason` stamp added to coaching notes will be silently dropped from the persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecar payload — contradicting the plan's claim that 'the annotation reaches the persisted verdict'.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:503-514
findings.suggested_fix: In `sidecar.py` `build_payload`, add `"triage": c.get("triage"), "triage_reason": c.get("triage_reason")` to the per-coaching-note dict. Also add `sidecar.py` to the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) expose `APPLY_NOW_MARGIN` as an env-var / config entry (consistent with T5e 'thresholds expected to evolve are configurable') so R7 tuning can land without a code PR, or (b) explicitly document in the plan why a module-constant-only approach is intentional and acceptable despite planned post-ship tuning.
evidence: Plan states: 'APPLY_NOW_MARGIN is a named module constant, default 0.10'
Plan states: 'validated post-ship by dogfooding via R7's advisory-application-latency metric' — explicitly expecting the threshold to be revisited
Plan states: 'No Markdown, no config flag' — explicitly closes off runtime/env configurability
findings.finding: APPLY_NOW_MARGIN is the one threshold the plan explicitly plans to tune post-ship (via R7 dogfood), yet the plan also explicitly rejects a config flag ('No config flag'), leaving it as a module-level constant that requires a code change + redeploy to adjust after R7 data arrives.
findings.location: ## What — 'APPLY_NOW_MARGIN is a named module constant, default 0.10' + '## Scope — passes.py' + 'No config flag' (last sentence of What)
scenarios: R7 dogfood shows 0.10 is too loose or too tight; tuning requires a code PR + review cycle rather than a config change, adding friction to the exact iteration loop the plan calls for.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-liner env-var override, e.g. `APPLY_NOW_MARGIN = float(os.environ.get('REBAR_APPLY_NOW_MARGIN', '0.10'))`, and note it in docs/plan-review-gate.md. This keeps 'no config flag' (no feature flag / runtime toggle) while still allowing ops-level tuning between deploys.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `APPLY_NOW_MARGIN` needs to be a module-level named constant vs. an inline default in `triage_advisories`'s signature (e.g., `margin: float = 0.10`) — the AC boundary test can import the function and vary the argument, so the named-constant form is not required for testability; if kept as a constant, note the Rule-of-Three gap (currently 1 call site).
evidence: Plan: 'APPLY_NOW_MARGIN is a named module constant, default 0.10'
Zero existing call sites for APPLY_NOW_MARGIN found in codebase (search confirmed no matches)
Only one planned consumer: triage_advisories in passes.py
ACs reference it by name ('APPLY_NOW_MARGIN' in grep command) but a function default parameter would equally satisfy this
findings.finding: The plan introduces `APPLY_NOW_MARGIN` as a module-level named constant in `passes.py` with exactly one call site (`triage_advisories`). The Rule-of-Three requires ≥3 call sites for a named abstraction to be non-premature; the plan cites none beyond the single triage function. The ACs reference it by name for boundary testing, but the constant could equally be an inline default in the function signature (e.g., `def triage_advisories(surviving, *, margin=0.10)`) and still be importable by the test — so the named-constant form adds no testability that a default parameter would not provide.
findings.location: ## What — 'APPLY_NOW_MARGIN is a named module constant, default 0.10'; ## Scope — passes.py
scenarios: A second team adds a different margin-based threshold elsewhere and reaches for APPLY_NOW_MARGIN thinking it applies generally, creating a silent semantic coupling.
findings.suggested_fix: Change to `def triage_advisories(surviving: list[dict], *, apply_now_margin: float = 0.10) -> list[dict]` and update the AC grep command to `grep -q 'apply_now_margin' tests/test_plan_review_triage.py` — or keep the constant but explicitly justify it as a future-facing configuration anchor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the 'Mechanism choice' section, explicitly state whether extending MOVE_REGISTRY_SCHEMA to support an aggregate-move type (multi-finding, no {subject} required) was evaluated and why the standalone pure-function path was preferred over that alternative.
evidence: SotA R6 (docs/research/task-decomposition-sota-2026.md §R6): 'Add a move to the registry (rendered deterministically, like all moves)'

Plan: 'It is NOT an entry in the Pass-4 MOVE_REGISTRY … The registry schema (MOVE_REGISTRY_SCHEMA) is a per-finding {subject}-placeholder template the LLM fills; a single {subject} substitution cannot express a ranked two-bucket split across all surviving advisories.'
MOVE_REGISTRY_SCHEMA in review_kernel/coach.py: template must carry a single {subject} placeholder — this constraint is real; the plan's technical claim is accurate

ABSENCE: the plan does not mention whether a new aggregate-move type (template without {subject}, multi-finding scope) was evaluated as an extension to the registry schema before choosing the standalone-function path
findings.finding: The plan diverges from its parent recommendation (SotA R6: 'Add a move to the registry') with a novel pure-function architectural path, justifying the divergence by the MOVE_REGISTRY's single-{subject}-per-finding schema constraint — but does not document whether extending the registry schema to support an 'aggregate' move type (multi-finding, no {subject}) was considered and rejected. Without that evaluation, a reader cannot tell if the novelty is forced or if a less novel schema extension would have served.
findings.location: What section — 'Mechanism choice (rationale)'
scenarios: A future developer building a second aggregate coaching feature (e.g. cross-finding pattern detection) finds no precedent in the registry for aggregate moves and duplicates the standalone-function pattern, increasing fragmentation.
A reviewer evaluating whether the architectural choice was necessary cannot reconstruct whether the registry-extension path was considered.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Mechanism choice rationale: 'An aggregate move type (schema extension allowing multi-finding templates without a {subject} slot) was considered but rejected because [reason — e.g. it would require changes to the shared kernel coach mechanism and the registry contract, which the plan is explicitly scoped to leave unchanged].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (with a code citation to `pass3_decide`'s return shape or the decided-finding dict contract in decide.py) that `block_threshold` is stamped on every decided finding dict, or else amend the Scope to add the criterion registry as an input to `triage_advisories`.
evidence: Plan: 'block_threshold (the criterion blocking waterline; always set on the decided finding — decide.py, 0.60 under plan-review's routing, DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD=0.95 otherwise)'
passes.py re-exports `pass3_decide` from `review_kernel/decide.py` — the field-stamping behavior of the returned dict is not confirmed by inspection in this review

Plan: 'No other field is used — there is NO "remediation-mode carryover status" field on Pass-4 findings' — this specificity implies the author verified field presence, but it is an unresolved capability assertion without a code citation to the decided-finding dict structure
findings.finding: The plan asserts `block_threshold` is 'always set on the decided finding' (citing decide.py) and uses it as a key ranking input to `triage_advisories`, but does not verify that `pass3_decide` actually stamps this field onto the returned finding dict. If `block_threshold` is only consulted transiently during the decision computation and not persisted onto the finding, `triage_advisories` would need to look it up from the criterion registry — an additional dependency the plan does not mention.
findings.location: What section — 'The ranking rule (fully specified, grounded in real fields)'
scenarios: `triage_advisories` is implemented reading `finding['block_threshold']` which is absent on the dict, causing a KeyError or silent fallback to DEFAULT_BLOCK_THRESHOLD for every finding — making all triage bucketing use the wrong threshold and producing incorrect apply-now/defer splits without test failures (tests would only catch this if they use realistic decided-finding dicts with `block_threshold` set).

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the output format/structure by which the triage stage's results are attached to the coaching output in the persisted verdict — an executor implementing `workflow_ops.py` must choose whether to append a Markdown block, add a structured JSON field, or both, with no stated default.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py`; What section — 'attached to the plan-review coaching output'

findings.finding: The `triage` stamp (values: `apply-now` | `defer`) added to coach notes and the `triage_reason` optional field are not specified as additions to any schema contract. The plan's existing `_pass4_model` CoachNote in passes.py has `move_id`, `subject`, `finding_refs` — no `triage` or `triage_reason` — and the plan does not say whether to add these fields to the Pass-4 output contract or treat them as post-render annotations.
findings.location: What section: 'each surviving-advisory coach note gains triage + optional triage_reason'; Scope: passes.py _pass4_model / render_coach_notes
findings.suggested_fix: Stamp triage post-render (analogous to the guide_url annotation in finalize_verdict lines 525-530), explicitly calling this out in the Scope section as 'triage stamped onto rendered notes after render_coach_notes, not part of the CoachNote LLM contract'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a canonical format template (e.g. a Python f-string or named format constant such as `DEFER_REASON_FMT = "deferred: priority {priority:.2f} is {gap:.2f} below its {block_threshold:.2f} block line"`) directly in the plan so both the implementation and the test assertion are unambiguously pinned.
evidence: Plan text: `deferred: priority 0.32 is 0.28 below its 0.60 block line — fixed words + recorded numbers, no LLM prose, non-prescriptive`
AC1: 'every `reason` string matches the locked format (only fixed tokens + recorded numeric fields — no LLM/prescriptive prose)'
ABSENCE: no explicit Python f-string or format template is given — the template must be inferred from the example
findings.finding: The defer-reason format is illustrated by a single example (`deferred: priority 0.32 is 0.28 below its 0.60 block line`) rather than a formal locked template string (e.g. `f"deferred: priority {priority:.2f} is {delta:.2f} below its {block_threshold:.2f} block line"`). AC1 asserts 'every `reason` string matches the locked format', but two independent implementors could produce non-byte-identical strings that both satisfy the prose description — making the determinism AC un-pinnable without a canonical template.
findings.location: ## What — 'Defer reason' bullet; AC1
scenarios: An executor writes `f"deferred: priority {priority} is {gap:.2f} below the {threshold} block line"` (different field name, no float formatting on priority) — the test author uses a different template; both satisfy the prose but the test fails.
The determinism AC cannot be implemented as a regex/exact-match assertion without a canonical template.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the 'Defer reason' bullet: `Locked template (Python): f"deferred: priority {priority:.2f} is {gap:.2f} below its {block_threshold:.2f} block line"` where `gap = block_threshold - priority`. Include this template in the `APPLY_NOW_MARGIN` constant block in `passes.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the plan title (or add a clarifying parenthetical) to accurately reflect that the triage is a standalone pure function on the Pass-4 path rather than a MOVE_REGISTRY entry, e.g. '(pass-4 coach path, ship-first)'.
evidence: Title: 'advisory-triage coaching move (pass-4 registry, ship-first)'

Body: 'It is NOT an entry in the Pass-4 MOVE_REGISTRY (in passes.py, re-exported by orchestrator.py). The registry schema … is a per-finding {subject}-placeholder template the LLM fills; a single {subject} substitution cannot express a ranked two-bucket split across all surviving advisories.'
findings.finding: The plan title includes '(pass-4 registry)' which implies the triage is implemented as a MOVE_REGISTRY entry, but the body explicitly states 'It is NOT an entry in the Pass-4 MOVE_REGISTRY' — the title directly contradicts the implementation mechanism, which could cause an implementer to place the logic in the wrong location.
findings.location: Title: 'advisory-triage coaching move (pass-4 registry, ship-first)' vs. ## What — 'It is NOT an entry in the Pass-4 MOVE_REGISTRY'
scenarios: An implementer reading only the title and Scope (passes.py, workflow_ops.py) might initially attempt to wire triage into MOVE_REGISTRY before reading the mechanism rationale, wasting effort or introducing incorrect structure.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title to: 'advisory-triage coaching stage (pass-4 coach path, ship-first)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC1 to explicitly state that `reason` is locked to `""` for `apply-now` entries and to the `deferred: priority {p} is {delta} below its {t} block line` template for `defer` entries, so the determinism test has an unambiguous format contract for both buckets.
evidence: AC1: 'every `reason` string matches the locked format (only fixed tokens + recorded numeric fields — no LLM/prescriptive prose)'
What/output shape: '`reason` is `""` for `apply-now`'
What/defer reason: 'Built only from the finding's own numbers, e.g. `deferred: priority 0.32 is 0.28 below its 0.60 block line`' — format only defined for deferred entries
findings.finding: AC1's determinism check says 'every `reason` string matches the locked format (only fixed tokens + recorded numeric fields)' but the What section specifies that `reason` is `""` (empty string) for `apply-now` entries — the only described locked format is the defer template (`deferred: priority X is Y below its Z block line`). An empty string does not match that format, so the AC as written would either fail on apply-now entries or require a separate locked format to be defined for them.
findings.location: AC1 (determinism) vs What section — output shape (`reason is ""` for apply-now) and defer-reason format description
scenarios: A test asserting locked-format compliance on all triage entries will need to special-case apply-now vs defer, but the AC does not say so — implementer and reviewer will disagree on whether `""` passes.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC1 to read: '…every `reason` string matches its locked format — `""` for `apply-now`, and `deferred: priority {p} is {delta} below its {block_threshold} block line` (fixed tokens + recorded numeric fields, no LLM prose) for `defer` entries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly in the plan (and in `triage_advisories`'s docstring) whether the function is order-independent (always re-sorts, regardless of input order) — this clarifies the redundancy with `partition_findings`'s existing priority sort and prevents a future maintainer from silently dropping the primary sort key on the assumption the input is already ordered.
evidence: orchestrator.py:443: `advisory.sort(key=lambda f: -float(f.get('priority', 0.0)))`
Plan: 'The triage array is sorted by priority DESC, then by criteria[0] ... then by id ASC'
The triage function receives 'surviving advisory findings' which are the post-cap surfaced list, already priority-sorted by partition_findings
findings.finding: `orchestrator.partition_findings` already sorts advisories by `priority` DESC (`advisory.sort(key=lambda f: -float(f.get('priority', 0.0)))` at orchestrator.py:443) before producing the `surfaced` list. The plan's `triage_advisories` receives this already-sorted list and independently re-sorts it with a superset key (priority DESC, criteria[0] ASC, id ASC). The plan does not acknowledge this implicit pre-sort dependency or explain why re-sorting is preferred over relying on the existing order. This creates a latent NIH-adjacent redundancy: the priority-DESC component of the triage sort duplicates work already done in `partition_findings`.
findings.location: ## What — 'Ordering (total, deterministic → byte-identical)'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:443
scenarios: If a future refactor changes partition_findings to not pre-sort (e.g., for a different cap strategy), triage_advisories silently continues to work correctly because it re-sorts — but the implementation author may assume pre-sorting and omit the primary sort key, breaking determinism when the input order changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'Mechanism choice' paragraph's negative framing ('It is NOT an entry in the MOVE_REGISTRY') with a brief affirmative placement statement (e.g., 'Implemented as a standalone pure function in `passes.py`, after `pass4_coach`, so it is co-located with the Pass-4 path it serves'), and move the rationale to a comment in the code rather than the plan spec.
evidence: Plan: 'It is NOT an entry in the Pass-4 MOVE_REGISTRY (in passes.py, re-exported by orchestrator.py). The registry schema (review_kernel/coach.py MOVE_REGISTRY_SCHEMA) is a per-finding {subject}-placeholder template the LLM fills; a single {subject} substitution cannot express a ranked two-bucket split across all surviving advisories.'
T8(e) criterion: 'No instruction-locality / pink-elephant antipatterns'
findings.finding: The 'Mechanism choice (rationale)' paragraph is classic pink-elephant / instruction-locality antipattern: it spends a full paragraph telling the implementer what NOT to build ('It is NOT an entry in the Pass-4 MOVE_REGISTRY … a single {subject} substitution cannot express a ranked two-bucket split'). This negatively-framed directive about the registry — embedded in the spec — risks drawing attention to the MOVE_REGISTRY as the place to look, and could cause a code-reviewer or future editor to re-litigate the placement decision unnecessarily. More concretely, it also means the spec never affirmatively names where in `passes.py` the function should be placed relative to the existing `pass4_coach` and `MOVE_REGISTRY` definitions, creating an instruction-locality gap.
findings.location: What § / 'Mechanism choice (rationale)' paragraph
scenarios: A future editor seeing the negative rationale but not the surrounding spec decides to add it to the registry anyway when the spec is unclear, reintroducing the architectural concern.
A reviewer focusing on the 'NOT MOVE_REGISTRY' wording spends time verifying that constraint rather than the positive correctness of the pure-function approach.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
